title: #9615 isn't fixed in 3.1.0 (containers eternally stopping if they do too much stdio in specific circumstances)
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
Containers stuck in "stopping" state on 3.1.0 (or crash on 3.0.1, #9615)
<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

Sorry the steps are convoluted, I tried to make a simple reproducer but I couldn't get it to work.

1. Clone https://gitlab.com/hackintosh5/riptester
2. pip install -r requirements.txt
3. In another window, `podman --log-level debug system service --time 0`
3. In the first window, `python -m backend`
4. In yet another window, `curl -X POST -H 'X-Api-Token=dff55903-db2c-4fd9-9348-e5fc9b0a599e' http://localhost:8080/v1/exec/python3 -d 'code=while 1:%0A print(0)&stdin='`
These steps always reproduce the issue on my machine (and might be useful for a test of #9615 if someone can work out exactly what is needed to reproduce it).

**Describe the results you received:**
Logs show:
```
INFO[0057] APIHandler(b94dba66-664a-4605-b26c-274ee1b6ffca) -- POST /v1.40/containers/e043f0c64304ba719b2d097ea1a22e215b9e02d7a0cbb13192839b4dc9be8ebf/stop BEGIN 
DEBU[0057] APIHandler(b94dba66-664a-4605-b26c-274ee1b6ffca) -- Header: Accept-Encoding=[gzip, deflate] 
DEBU[0057] APIHandler(b94dba66-664a-4605-b26c-274ee1b6ffca) -- Header: User-Agent=[Python/3.9 aiohttp/3.7.4.post0] 
DEBU[0057] APIHandler(b94dba66-664a-4605-b26c-274ee1b6ffca) -- Header: Content-Length=[0] 
DEBU[0057] APIHandler(b94dba66-664a-4605-b26c-274ee1b6ffca) -- Header: Content-Type=[application/octet-stream] 
DEBU[0057] APIHandler(b94dba66-664a-4605-b26c-274ee1b6ffca) -- Header: Accept=[*/*] 
DEBU[0057] Starting parallel job on container e043f0c64304ba719b2d097ea1a22e215b9e02d7a0cbb13192839b4dc9be8ebf 
DEBU[0057] Stopping ctr e043f0c64304ba719b2d097ea1a22e215b9e02d7a0cbb13192839b4dc9be8ebf (timeout 1) 
DEBU[0057] Stopping container e043f0c64304ba719b2d097ea1a22e215b9e02d7a0cbb13192839b4dc9be8ebf (PID 10736) 
DEBU[0057] Sending signal 15 to container e043f0c64304ba719b2d097ea1a22e215b9e02d7a0cbb13192839b4dc9be8ebf 
INFO[0058] Timed out stopping container e043f0c64304ba719b2d097ea1a22e215b9e02d7a0cbb13192839b4dc9be8ebf, resorting to SIGKILL: given PIDs did not die within timeout 
DEBU[0058] Sending signal 9 to container e043f0c64304ba719b2d097ea1a22e215b9e02d7a0cbb13192839b4dc9be8ebf 
INFO[0063] Request Failed(Internal Server Error): given PIDs did not die within timeout 
DEBU[0063] APIHandler(b94dba66-664a-4605-b26c-274ee1b6ffca) -- POST /v1.40/containers/e043f0c64304ba719b2d097ea1a22e215b9e02d7a0cbb13192839b4dc9be8ebf/stop END 
```
Podman inspect (truncated) shows:
```
        "State": {
            "OciVersion": "1.0.2-dev",
            "Status": "stopping",
            "Running": false,
            "Paused": false,
            "Restarting": false,
            "OOMKilled": false,
            "Dead": false,
            "Pid": 10736,
            "ConmonPid": 10733,
            "ExitCode": 0,
            "Error": "",
            "StartedAt": "2021-04-08T16:11:37.478418293+01:00",
            "FinishedAt": "0001-01-01T00:00:00Z",
            "Healthcheck": {
                "Status": "",
                "FailingStreak": 0,
                "Log": null
            }
        },
```
(note the container is stuck in the stopping state)

**Describe the results you expected:**
Container is stopped cleanly by SIGTERM (Python should exit immediately when it gets a SIGTERM)

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
The issue can't be reproduced unless the application is doing a lot of output - perhaps some internal buffer gets full, deadlocking signals somehow? just speculation.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.1.0
API Version:  3.1.0
Go Version:   go1.15.8
Built:        Mon Apr  5 15:21:04 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
podman-3.1.0-2.fc33.x86_64
```
Installed from https://koji.fedoraproject.org/koji/buildinfo?buildID=1732440 because 3.0.1 (latest version available in Fedora stable) is subject to #9615

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes (troubleshooting is a [dead link](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/cmd/podman/README.md))

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Fedora 33 cgroupsv2 rootless

comments.body:
Just noticed - the root process of the container becomes a zombie according to procfs. Killing conmon (the parent) with SIGCHLD makes no difference - it just stays asleep.
```
Name:	python
State:	Z (zombie)
Tgid:	12295
Ngid:	0
Pid:	12295
PPid:	12292
```

`INFO[0063] Request Failed(Internal Server Error): given PIDs did not die within timeout `

I'm fairly certain this means our zombie is such that even SIGKILL won't get rid of it. At this point, it's out of our hands - Podman can't do anything further about the process since even the kernel can't kill it. If you want to verify by hitting the zombie with a SIGKILL it would be appreciated, but I'm quite sure this is the case.

The Podman bug here is that we aren't moving the container to the `stopped` state. I don't know if we can move to exited (that involved unmounting the container, and we can't do that if the filesystem is in use, and there's a zombie using the filesystem that we can't kill... But we can move to the "stopped" state, which should allow the container to be used again.

> `INFO[0063] Request Failed(Internal Server Error): given PIDs did not die within timeout `
> 
> I'm fairly certain this means our zombie is such that even SIGKILL won't get rid of it. At this point, it's out of our hands - Podman can't do anything further about the process since even the kernel can't kill it. If you want to verify by hitting the zombie with a SIGKILL it would be appreciated, but I'm quite sure this is the case.
> 
> The Podman bug here is that we aren't moving the container to the `stopped` state. I don't know if we can move to exited (that involved unmounting the container, and we can't do that if the filesystem is in use, and there's a zombie using the filesystem that we can't kill... But we can move to the "stopped" state, which should allow the container to be used again.

The process is just Python. Moreover, I found a really simple reproducer that might help find the cause of the issue. In any case, the issue is 100% a bug in Podman (or something related) since sending SIGKILL to Python should Just Work :tm: . Will attach my reproducer in a moment.

```python
import aiodocker, asyncio

async def main():
    docker = aiodocker.Docker(url="unix:///run/user/1000/podman/podman.sock")
    c = await docker.containers.create({"Image": "python:3", "Cmd": ["python", "-c", "while 1:\n print(1)"], "HostConfig": {"LogConfig": {"Type": "none"}}})
    conn = c.attach(stdout=True)
    await c.start()
    await conn.read_out()
    await conn.close()
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    await c.kill()
asyncio.run(main())
```
This code will cause process killing to hang forever, despite the process actually being a zombie. The python code is doing nothing more complex than printing "1". My guess is that conmon is waiting until the process has a clean stdout before it cleans up the process, leading to this hang when the reader process hangs up.

are you able to reproduce it without remote podman?

I am trying something as:

```
$ podman run --rm -ti python:3 python -c "while 1: print(1)"
```

and from another terminal do the `kill -9 $PYTHON_PROCESS_IN_THE_CONTAINER`

the process is correctly reported as terminated and podman terminates.

Does the simpler version work for you?

> are you able to reproduce it without remote podman?
> 
> I am trying something as:
> 
> ```
> $ podman run --rm -ti python:3 python -c "while 1: print(1)"
> ```
> 
> and from another terminal do the `kill -9 $PYTHON_PROCESS_IN_THE_CONTAINER`
> 
> the process is correctly reported as terminated and podman terminates.
> 
> Does the simpler version work for you?

I wasn't able to reproduce with cli only, but I don't know why.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@mheon Any more thoughts on this, Did you make the changes?
Nope, not as of present.
Would still help to have a Conmon expert look more at this, too.
@giuseppe @haircommander @mrunalp PTAL
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Still an issue

I can take a look.
